variants.text:
E1_W1_L1 — The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 1
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework
Talking Point: Welcome to ASEN 3501. Before we touch a single piece of hardware or write a single line of MATLAB, I want to establish the professional identity this course is building toward and the framework that organizes everything we will do. You are training to be a test engineer. Test engineers design tests, characterize systems, and validate models. The AMVF is the framework test engineers use, uncertainty is the language test engineers speak, and the ability to argue for a result numerically and with bounded confidence is the output test engineers produce. That identity stake starts today, and I will echo it at the opening of every lab segment this semester.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Two Questions This Course Answers
(Image: A bold typographic slide with two large questions centered on a dark background: "Is this prediction right?" and "Will this do what we want?" Below each question, a thin line connects to a contrasting pair of icons — a glowing computer simulation display (CFD contour plot in blue and red) on the left, and a physical test article mounted on a stand with sensors attached on the right. Between the two icons, a thick double-headed arrow labeled "Experimental Methods." The overall visual is dramatic and professional — the questions are the largest text on the slide.)
Talking Point: These two questions are the organizing logic of everything you will do in this course and, I would argue, a significant fraction of what you will be paid to do as aerospace engineers. Your other coursework builds your capacity to generate predictions — to say "based on this model, the system should behave like this." That skill is genuinely valuable. But prediction without validation is a guess with better vocabulary. The skill this course builds is the second half: using physical reality to check whether the prediction holds, quantifying how closely it holds, and knowing what to do when it does not. By the time you leave this course, you will be able to answer both questions with a number, a confidence interval, and a defensible argument.

================================================================================

slide 3: You Are Training to Be a Test Engineer
(Image: A professional three-node triangle diagram with labeled roles. Bottom-left node: "Analyst/Modeler" — icon of equations on a whiteboard. Bottom-right node: "Instrumentation Engineer" — icon of a sensor connected to a DAQ system. Top node (gold, larger): "Test Engineer" — icon of a person at a test stand with a clipboard. Bidirectional arrows connect all three nodes. Below the triangle, three one-line descriptors aligned under the top node: "designs tests," "characterizes systems," "validates models." A caption below: "This is what ASEN 3501 builds." Clean, professional style on white background with gold accent.)
Talking Point: A test engineer is not someone who just runs experiments and records numbers — that is a technician. A test engineer is someone who reads a mathematical model, asks what that model is claiming about physical reality, designs a test that can confirm or refute that claim with quantified confidence, instruments it properly, propagates uncertainty through the data reduction, and produces a defensible conclusion. That is the full stack, and every activity in this course is practicing some piece of it. In Phase I you will walk the measurement and comparison steps. In Phase II you will add the predictive modeling and pre-commitment steps. In Phase III you will own the design from the beginning. All of it is test engineering. All of it is what employers will hire you to do.

================================================================================

slide 4: The AMVF — Two Branches, One Validation Goal
(Image: Clean AMVF flowchart on white background with gold highlights. Two parallel vertical branches. Left branch labeled "Mathematical Modeling Branch" — nodes top to bottom: System/Phenomenon of Interest → Conceptual Model → Mathematical Model → Computational Model → Simulation Outcomes. Right branch labeled "Physical Modeling Branch" — nodes top to bottom: System/Phenomenon of Interest → Physical Realization → Experimental Measurement → Data Reduction → Experimental Outcomes. Both branches share the top node and converge at the bottom on a node labeled "Quantitative Comparison." Below Quantitative Comparison: two labeled arrows — left arrow "Validation" pointing back up to Mathematical Model, right arrow "Experimentally Informed Model" with a feedback loop icon. Uncertainty quantification is shown as a vertical bar spanning both branches. Clean and high-contrast.)
Talking Point: This is the Aerospace Modeling and Validation Framework — the AMVF — and it is the organizing spine of this course. The left branch is what your other classes develop: you take a physical phenomenon, build a conceptual model, translate it into mathematics, run a simulation, and produce a prediction. The right branch is what this course teaches: you take the same phenomenon, design a physical realization of it, instrument and measure it carefully, reduce the data, propagate uncertainty, and produce an experimental outcome. Validation is what happens when both branches meet at the bottom of this diagram. The critical insight is that neither branch is complete without the other — a simulation without experimental grounding is an educated guess, and data without a model to compare against is just observation. The meeting point is where engineering credibility lives.

================================================================================

slide 5: Verification vs. Validation — Not the Same Thing
(Image: Two clearly separated panels side by side with a bold vertical divider. Left panel, labeled "Verification" with a blue checkmark icon: title text "Are we solving the equations correctly?" Below, a code snippet on a screen being compared to an analytical reference solution. Caption: "Internal consistency — does the code do what the math says?" Right panel, labeled "Validation" with a gold target-and-arrow icon: title text "Are we solving the right equations?" Below, a graph showing a model prediction curve overlaid with experimental data points and error bars. Caption: "External correspondence — does the model represent physical reality?" Below both panels: "You can have a perfectly verified simulation and a completely invalid model.")
Talking Point: Verification and validation are two distinct activities that get conflated constantly, and I want to be precise about this from Day 1. Verification asks whether we are solving the equations correctly — it is a question about numerical implementation, about whether the code faithfully represents the mathematics. Validation asks whether we are solving the right equations — whether the mathematical model actually corresponds to the physical phenomenon it claims to describe. You can write flawless, bug-free code that perfectly implements a model, and if that model has wrong physics in it, you have a perfectly verified, completely invalid simulation. This course lives almost entirely in the validation territory. The right branch of the AMVF is how we gather the physical evidence needed to answer the validation question.

================================================================================

slide 6: Outcomes vs. Results — A Distinction You Will Use Every Week
(Image: A vertical flow diagram on a light background. Four steps shown top to bottom, connected by arrows. Step 1: "Raw Sensor Output" — a jagged noisy voltage-vs-time trace with axis labels. Step 2: "Data Reduction" — a small MATLAB code snippet showing uncertainty propagation calculation. Step 3: "Result" — a single numerical value with units: "9.76 m/s²." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Outcome" — the value with full uncertainty statement: "9.76 ± 0.14 m/s² (95% CI, partial derivative method)." A red annotation arrow points from Step 3 to Step 4 with the label "This step is what makes comparison possible." A second branch from Step 4 points right to "Quantitative Comparison" with a model value shown.)
Talking Point: The AMVF maps physical measurements to experimental outcomes — not experimental results. These are not the same thing. A result is a number that comes out of your data reduction: 9.76 meters per second squared. An outcome is a result that also carries its uncertainty, a stated confidence level, and a method statement — 9.76 plus or minus 0.14 meters per second squared at 95% confidence, computed by partial derivative propagation. The distinction matters because the outcome is what allows comparison. Without the uncertainty bounds, I cannot determine whether 9.76 agrees with a model that predicts 9.81. With the bounds, I can make a defensible quantitative argument. From this point forward, every numerical claim you make in this course must be expressed as an outcome.

================================================================================

slide 7: Uncertainty Is Mandatory — Here Is Why
(Image: A horizontal number line showing a model prediction at a fixed point — vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction: 42.3 N·m." Two experimental outcomes are shown as horizontal bars indicating uncertainty ranges. Outcome A: narrow bar centered at 44.1, labeled "44.1 ± 0.4 N·m — gap exceeds uncertainty, significant discrepancy." The bar does not overlap the model prediction line. Outcome B: wider bar also centered at 44.1, labeled "44.1 ± 2.8 N·m — agreement within uncertainty." This bar overlaps the model prediction line. Below both: "Same measured value — completely different engineering conclusions." A bold caption: "Uncertainty is not a footnote. It is the argument.")
Talking Point: Here is the most important visual in this lecture. Both outcomes show the same measured value — 44.1 Newton-meters. The model predicts 42.3. Do they agree? The answer depends entirely on the uncertainty. In Outcome A, the uncertainty is tiny — the gap between 44.1 and 42.3 is much larger than the bounds, and I have a significant discrepancy I need to explain. In Outcome B, the uncertainty is larger — the gap falls within the bounds, and agreement is defensible. The same number, measured by the same instrument, leads to completely different engineering conclusions depending on the uncertainty. This is why uncertainty is not optional in this course, and it is why professional standards like ASME V&V 10-2006 require it. Uncertainty is not a sign of weakness. It is the argument.

================================================================================

slide 8: Where Phase I Sits in the AMVF
(Image: The full AMVF flowchart from slide 4, now with a gold shaded overlay covering the Physical Modeling Branch nodes (Physical Realization, Experimental Measurement, Data Reduction, Experimental Outcomes) and the Quantitative Comparison node. The Mathematical Modeling Branch is grayed out with a label "Given for now." A large bracket on the right side labels the shaded region "Phase I — Weeks 1–5: Execution & Quantification." Smaller annotations mark subsequent phases along the left branch: "Phase II — Weeks 6–10: Add predictive modeling" and "Phase III — Weeks 11–15: Design from scratch." The progression left-to-right in the AMVF corresponds to increasing student ownership.)
Talking Point: For the next five weeks — Phase I of this course — your job is to walk the right branch of the AMVF with discipline. The mathematical model is given to you. Your job is to take the physical phenomenon, design and execute the measurement, reduce the data, propagate your uncertainty, and produce an experimental outcome that can be compared to the model prediction. You are not designing the experiment from scratch yet — that is Phase III. You are not developing the mathematical model independently — that is Phase II. But Phase I is not just a warmup. The physical modeling branch is where experimental credibility is earned or lost. A perfectly good mathematical model cannot be validated by a carelessly executed experiment. Phase I builds the foundation everything else rests on.

================================================================================

slide 9: The AMVF Connects to Professional Engineering Standards
(Image: The AMVF flowchart shown as a central hub with three satellite documents connected by arrows labeled "governed by." Satellite 1 (top): ASME V&V 10-2006 — shown as a formal document cover with subtitle "Guide for V&V in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer." Satellite 2 (bottom-left): AIAA Measurement Standards — shown as a document with the AIAA logo placeholder. Satellite 3 (bottom-right): ASME PTC Measurement Uncertainty Standards — shown as a document cover. Below all three: a caption reading "The AMVF is not a course diagram. It is a professional framework grounded in published standards." Clean professional layout.)
Talking Point: The AMVF is not something invented for this class. It reflects a framework that appears throughout professional aerospace and mechanical engineering and that is grounded in published engineering standards. The primary anchor for this course is ASME V&V 10-2006 — the American Society of Mechanical Engineers' Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer. You will be assigned a reading on this document this week, and it will be a reference throughout the course. The other standards on this slide — AIAA measurement standards, ASME measurement uncertainty standards — will be cited as we progress through the semester, and by Phase III you will be required to reference them in your V&V plan. Professional engineers do not make up their validation methodology from scratch — they anchor it to standards that the broader community recognizes.

================================================================================

slide 10: The Experimentally Informed Model
(Image: A cyclical three-node loop diagram. Node 1 (top): "Mathematical Model" — an equation displayed, e.g., F = ma with some terms highlighted. Arrow down-right to Node 2 (right): "Experiment" — a test article with sensors, arrows labeled "predicts expected outcome." Arrow down-left to Node 3 (bottom): "Experimental Outcome" — a graph with data points and uncertainty bars alongside a model line, labeled "measured, with uncertainty." Arrow up-left back to Node 1, labeled "informs and updates model." The loop conveys iteration. Inset in the lower right corner: a small "before/after" graphic showing a model prediction curve (dashed) that initially misses the data, then an updated curve (solid) that passes through the data within uncertainty — labeled "Experimentally Informed Model.")
Talking Point: There is a common misconception I want to address on the first day: the goal of an experiment is not to confirm the model. The goal is to learn from the comparison. When your experimental outcome disagrees with the model prediction, that discrepancy is not a failure — it is information. It tells you what physics the model is missing, or what physical effect you did not account for in your experimental setup. The most valuable output of the right branch of the AMVF is not a verdict of "confirmed" or "rejected" — it is an experimentally informed model, one that has been updated based on what the data revealed. This concept runs through all five weeks of E1 and becomes the central deliverable in E2 and E3. In the first lab session this week, you will begin designing your canned experiment with this outcome explicitly in mind.

================================================================================

slide 11: What the Next Five Weeks Look Like
(Image: A horizontal five-column table, one column per week. Week 1: "Framework + Design" — AMVF icon, experiment design icon. Week 2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma icon, MATLAB icon. Week 3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two curves compared icon, validation metric symbol. Week 4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon showing all steps connected. Week 5: "Cross-Prediction + Transition" — two students exchanging experiment packets icon, bridge icon. Beneath each column: the primary deliverable for that week in smaller text. A gold arc labeled "Phase I: Execution and Quantification" spans all five columns. Each week's column is visually connected to the next by a right-pointing arrow.)
Talking Point: Here is the Phase I arc at a glance. This week you design your experiment and annotate an AMVF diagram. Next week you acquire the mathematical tools for uncertainty quantification — partial derivatives, Monte Carlo, confidence intervals — and begin collecting data on your assigned canned experiment. In Week 3 you learn how to make a quantitative comparison argument using a named validation metric. Week 4 integrates everything into a complete pipeline from hypothesis to AMVF annotation — that is the biggest report of E1. Then in Week 5 you do something that changes how you think about experiments: before anyone releases the data, you construct a predictive model for an experiment you did not run and commit to a prediction in writing. That is the cognitive bridge into Phase II. Each week builds directly on the last — and you cannot skip steps.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
The Aerospace Modeling and Validation Framework is not a diagram you memorize and move on from. It is the decision-making structure that a test engineer returns to every time they need to argue that a result is credible — when they design an experiment, when they argue for or against agreement between data and model, and when they plan a V&V strategy for a complex system. Today you have seen where this course sits in that structure, why verification and validation are different things, and why uncertainty is not optional but mandatory. Over the next fifteen weeks you will internalize all of it — not by memorizing the framework but by using it, week after week, to make defensible engineering claims about physical systems. That is the skill employers pay for. That is what we are building together. Welcome to Aerospace Experimental Methods.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- Test engineer professional identity framing planted prominently in slides 1, 3, and 12 per blueprint instruction.
- ASME V&V 10-2006 named explicitly in slides 7 and 9 per instructor_clarification_notes change #12.
- "Experimentally informed model" introduced in slide 10 per change #5.
- AMVF two-branch structure, verification vs. validation, outcomes vs. results all grounded in Conceptual_Arc.md and Lecture_Layout_iteration1.md.
- Slide count: 12 (title + 10 content + closure). All content slides have image descriptions. Closure has no image description.

Over the next fifteen weeks you will internalize all of it — not by memorizing the framework but by using it, week after week, to make defensible engineering claims about physical systems. That is the skill employers pay for. That is what we are building together. Welcome to Aerospace Experimental Methods.

================================================================================

variants.text:
E1_W1_L2 — Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design
Talking Point: In the first lecture, I introduced the AMVF and showed you where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go deeper into what measurement actually is. Not how to press buttons on a sensor, but what a sensor is really doing: what it reports, what it infers, and what it silently assumes. And in the second half of this lecture, I'll introduce the formal vocabulary of experiment design — the language you will use in your Phase 1 design deliverable this week when you commit to a hypothesis and a procedure for your assigned canned experiment before any data collection begins.

================================================================================

slide 2: Measurement Is Model Realization
(Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a physical system — a rotating wheel with angular velocity arrows, labeled "True Angular Velocity: ω." Bottom layer labeled "Sensor Output" shows a voltage-vs-time trace with visible noise and a slight baseline offset, labeled "Recorded Signal: 2.34 V ± noise." Between the two layers, a downward arrow passes through a gray box labeled with four stages: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Analog-to-Digital Conversion → Calibration Equation. A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.")
Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain.

================================================================================

slide 3: The Transducer Chain
(Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.)
Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause.

================================================================================

slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer
(Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?")
Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty.

================================================================================

slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies
(Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.)
Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment.

================================================================================

slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source
(Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.")
Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement.

================================================================================

slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not
(Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.")
Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a correction equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model.

================================================================================

slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference
(Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. The central horizontal spine points to a box labeled "Measured Outcome." Four angled branches extend from the spine, each labeled: "Environment" (sub-causes: temperature drift, humidity, ambient vibration), "Instrumentation" (sub-causes: sensor drift, noise floor, calibration age), "Operator" (sub-causes: positioning variability, recording inconsistency, timing errors), "Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.")
Talking Point: A confounding factor is anything that causes your dependent variable to change for reasons other than the independent variable you're manipulating. If you're measuring the period of a pendulum while someone opens a window, and the airflow changes the drag on the pendulum bob, that's a confound. If you're measuring structural deflection and the temperature in the lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent apparent effects that have nothing to do with the phenomenon you are studying. The antidote to confounding is experimental design: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order, and careful isolation of the variable you are trying to manipulate. One of the required elements of your Phase 1 design deliverable this week is to identify at least one confounding factor in your assigned experiment before you run it — because after you have collected the data is the wrong time to remember them.

================================================================================

slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary
(Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. Card 1: "Hypothesis" — template text: "If [independent variable] is changed by [specific amount or direction], then [dependent variable] will [specific expected response], because [physical reasoning in one sentence]." Card 2: "Variables" — a three-column table with column headers: Independent Variable (what you change), Dependent Variable (what you measure), Controlled Variables (what you hold fixed). Card 3: "Confounders" — a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note reading "All three elements are required in the Phase 1 design deliverable this week." The cards are visually distinct with subtle color coding.)
Talking Point: Formal experiment design requires specific vocabulary, and I want to give you that vocabulary precisely. A hypothesis is a specific, falsifiable, causal statement — it is not a question, it is not a guess, and it is not a vague expectation. An independent variable is the quantity you intentionally change. A dependent variable is the quantity you measure in response. Controlled variables are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three elements define the scope of the experiment — they tell you and everyone who reads your report what you were actually testing. This week's Phase 1 design deliverable requires all three, along with a hypothesis and at least one identified confounder. Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows.

================================================================================

slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison
(Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.)
Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction.

================================================================================

slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF
(Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.")
Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- References "assigned canned experiment" and "Phase 1 design deliverable" per Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example.
- P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early.
- Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced lightly in Slide 7 — tagged as "Week 3" for the deeper treatment, per blueprint continuity.
- Slide 11 explicitly connects to the annotated AMVF deliverable from the W1 lab session.
- Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint.
- Slide count: 12 (title + 10 content + closure). All content slides have image descriptions. Closure has no image description.

Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows.

================================================================================

slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison
(Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.)
Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction.

================================================================================

slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF
(Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.")
Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- References "assigned canned experiment" and "Phase 1 design deliverable" per Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example.
- P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early.
- Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced lightly in Slide 7 — tagged as "Week 3" for the deeper treatment, per blueprint continuity.
- Slide 11 explicitly connects to the annotated AMVF deliverable from the W1 lab session.
- Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint.
- Slide count: 12 (title + 10 content + closure). All content slides have image descriptions. Closure has no image description.

# AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation
## ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework

Every claim an engineer makes about a system is only as credible as the framework they used to make it — and professional experimentalists have spent decades building that framework already.

---

The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework introduced in Week 1's lecture is not an invention of this course. It is a synthesis of engineering practice that has been codified over decades in standards documents, measurement handbooks, and validation guides produced by professional organizations whose job is to decide how engineers should argue for results. This reading is a map to those resources — not a reading list you must complete now, but a set of pointers that will become required references as the course progresses.

The most important of these documents is **ASME V&V 10-2006**, the primary engineering standard anchoring the AMVF framework used throughout ASEN 3501. Published by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, V&V 10-2006 establishes the conceptual and procedural framework for verification and validation in computational engineering. It defines the terms — verification, validation, uncertainty, model adequacy — in the precise, technical sense that practicing engineers use. When the Week 1 lecture distinguished *verification* (did you solve the equations correctly?) from *validation* (are your equations the right ones for this problem?), those definitions trace directly to this document. When the AMVF flowchart separates mathematical modeling from physical modeling and places quantitative comparison at the intersection, that architecture reflects the V&V 10-2006 framework. This standard is the language primer for everything that follows in this course.

<image description: A two-column diagram showing the V&V framework structure. On the left, a "Mathematical Model" branch descends through equation derivation and numerical solution, terminating at "Verification." On the right, a "Physical Model" branch descends through experiment design, data collection, and uncertainty quantification, terminating at "Experimental Outcomes." A horizontal bridge labeled "Validation" connects the two branches at the bottom, with a bidirectional arrow labeled "Quantitative Comparison" spanning the gap. The overall structure mirrors the AMVF flowchart. A label in the lower corner reads "ASME V&V 10-2006." Clean black-on-white engineering diagram style, minimal shading, clear label typography.>

Beyond V&V 10-2006, practicing aerospace experimentalists draw on a cluster of related documents. The **AIAA measurement standards** — produced by the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics — address measurement methodology specific to aerospace applications: wind tunnel testing, flight measurements, and propulsion characterization. These standards specify how uncertainty should be treated in measurement-intensive aerospace environments and will surface explicitly when E2's more complex experimental setups are introduced. The key point is that measurement standards are not generic — different engineering domains have domain-specific standards that define acceptable practice, and knowing which standard applies to your experiment is part of experimental design.

For uncertainty analysis specifically, the **ASME PTC (Performance Test Codes) standards** provide procedural requirements for measurement uncertainty in engineering performance testing. These standards require that every reported result carry explicit uncertainty bounds derived by methods consistent with the standard's prescribed framework. If you are ever asked whether your measurement result is credible in a professional or regulatory context, the answer lives in whether your uncertainty analysis conforms to a recognized standard.

<image description: A layered pyramid diagram with three tiers. The top tier is labeled "Primary Standards" and shows ASME V&V 10-2006 and AIAA measurement standards as formal document icons. The middle tier is labeled "Reference Texts" and shows Coleman & Steele as a textbook icon alongside the ISO GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) as a second document icon. The bottom tier is labeled "Practitioner Tools" and shows icons for lab documentation, MATLAB, and data recording. Arrows point downward from each tier to the tier below, indicating that the upper layers define the rules that lower-layer practice must follow. Flat-design illustration style, CU Boulder gold and black color palette.>

The reference that bridges all of these to classroom practice is **Coleman & Steele**, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers* (4th edition). This book is the accessible bridge between the formal standards and the methods you will use in this course — beginning in Week 2, when partial derivative error propagation and Monte Carlo simulation are introduced. Relevant chapters will be assigned as the course progresses; having the book as a reference from Week 1 means you can follow the lecture directly into its theoretical foundations whenever you want to go deeper.

The professional implication is easy to miss on first encounter: when you apply the AMVF in a lab report, you are not following a course-specific convention — you are operating within the same framework that engineers at NASA, aerospace contractors, and national labs use when they are required to document that a test was conducted correctly. Standards documents are the mechanism by which that framework is enforced across organizations and over time. ASME V&V 10-2006 will be a required reference in E3 when you develop your own V&V plan. The AIAA and ASME PTC standards will surface in E2. Knowing they exist — and having a sense of what they contain — changes how you read your own results.

---

**The Takeaway:** ASME V&V 10-2006 is the professional anchor for the framework this course builds on — not a textbook chapter, but the actual document that practicing engineers cite when arguing that a test was done right. From Week 1, you are working within that professional tradition, not just a course format.

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# GitHub Basics for Course Documentation
## Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It

An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened — and in professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work.

---

Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence.

**GitHub** is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control — a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you will use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history.

<image description: A screenshot-style illustration of a GitHub repository page for a hypothetical student project called "ASEN3501-Portfolio." The repository shows a README.md file rendered at the top with a brief description: "ASEN 3501 experiment documentation — Weeks 1–15." Below the README, a file tree shows folders labeled "E1_week_1/", "E1_week_2/", and files including "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." The interface uses GitHub's actual visual style — white background, dark top navigation bar, file tree with icons — rendered as a clean flat illustration rather than a screenshot. A small commit message is visible below the file tree: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 experimental design and AMVF diagram."">

The mechanics are straightforward. A **repository** (repo) is a folder with version history — a project folder that remembers every saved version. A **README.md** is a text file written in **Markdown** — a lightweight formatting language — that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You will write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers (`# Title`), bold (`**term**`), and lists (`- item`) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser.

The workflow for adding files has three steps: *stage* (select which files to include in this snapshot), *commit* (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and *push* (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you will not need the full depth of version control — branches, pull requests, merge conflicts — but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you are explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now.

<image description: A three-step horizontal workflow diagram. Step 1 is labeled "Stage" and shows a folder icon with two files highlighted — "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." Step 2 is labeled "Commit" with a text bubble showing the message: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 design: hypothesis, variables, AMVF diagram." Step 3 is labeled "Push" with an upward arrow pointing to a cloud icon labeled "GitHub repository." Arrows connect each step left to right. Flat-design icons, CU Boulder gold accent color on the step labels, black and white otherwise.>

Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file — which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps — this is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate.

One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for final products. A repository with one massive commit at the end of the semester tells no story. Commits made as work progresses — small, frequent, with clear messages — create a record that shows how your thinking developed. That process record is a professional artifact. Engineers and employers who look at your GitHub portfolio are reading your technical judgment over time, not just the last thing you submitted.

---

**The Takeaway:** The Tier 1 Phase 1 pre-lab work from this week's design session is your first portfolio entry — commit it to your repository with a clear description, and begin the documentation habit that every subsequent lab deliverable will extend.

lectures.title: L01

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data.

================================================================================

slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same
(Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.")
Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester.

================================================================================

slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise
(Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.")
Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions.

================================================================================

slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random
(Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.)
Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected.

================================================================================

slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim
(Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed vertical arrow spanning ±0.4 N, labeled "Uncertainty Interval." To the right: a formal statement box reading "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Below the bar: a contrast pair. Left: a bare number "14.7 N" with a red label "Not an engineering claim — cannot be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." A definition box at the bottom: "Uncertainty: a quantified bound on how large the error could plausibly be, expressed as a probability interval.")
Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method.

================================================================================

slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions
(Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.)
Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why.

================================================================================

slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean
(Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.")
Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo.

================================================================================

slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility
(Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.")
Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result.

================================================================================

slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice
(Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.")
Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks.

================================================================================

slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows
(Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.)
Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool.

================================================================================
