E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data.

================================================================================

slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same
(Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.")
Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester.

================================================================================

slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise
(Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.")
Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions.

================================================================================

slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random
(Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.)
Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected.

================================================================================

slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim
(Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed vertical arrow spanning ±0.4 N, labeled "Uncertainty Interval." To the right: a formal statement box reading "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Below the bar: a contrast pair. Left: a bare number "14.7 N" with a red label "Not an engineering claim — cannot be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." A definition box at the bottom: "Uncertainty: a quantified bound on how large the error could plausibly be, expressed as a probability interval.")
Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method.

================================================================================

slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions
(Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.)
Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why.

================================================================================

slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean
(Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.")
Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo.

================================================================================

slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility
(Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.")
Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result.

================================================================================

slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice
(Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.")
Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks.

================================================================================

slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows
(Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.)
Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool.

================================================================================

variants.text:
E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities
(Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.")
Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively.

================================================================================

slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method
(Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.)
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course.

================================================================================

slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms
(Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.")
Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one.

================================================================================

slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest
(Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.")
Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly.

================================================================================

slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break
(Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.")
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

================================================================================

slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions
(Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.")
Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis.

================================================================================

slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge
(Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.")
Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward.

================================================================================

slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework
(Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.")
Talking Point: I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward.

================================================================================

slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example
(Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.")
Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes

- Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3.
- P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction.
- Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint.
- Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint.
- "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix.
- Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12).

E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities
(Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.")
Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively.

================================================================================

slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method
(Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.)
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course.

================================================================================

slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms
(Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.")
Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one.

================================================================================

slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest
(Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.")
Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly.

================================================================================

(Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.")
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

================================================================================

slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions
(Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.")
Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis.

================================================================================

If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward.

================================================================================

(Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. variants.text:
# Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition
## A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously

The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution — not a number — your entire relationship with experimental data changes.

---

This reading introduces a reference text you will use repeatedly throughout this course: *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers* by Hugh W. Coleman and W. Glenn Steele, Fourth Edition. It is not a cover-to-cover assignment. It is a field guide — different chapters address different problems you will encounter, and this reading orients you to the book's structure so you can find what you need when you need it.

Coleman & Steele belongs to a tradition of engineering texts that treat measurement uncertainty not as an afterthought — the error bar you add before submitting a figure — but as the primary product of an experiment. The core thesis of the book matches the core thesis of this course: the goal of an experiment is not to produce a number, it is to produce a defensible claim. A defensible claim requires not just a result but a quantified bound on how uncertain you are about that result, derived by methods that a critical reader can evaluate and reproduce.

<image description: A two-panel illustration. Left panel shows a single point on a number line labeled "reported value: 9.81 m/s²" — clean, falsely precise, with no additional information. Right panel shows a bell curve centered on 9.81 m/s² with shaded tails labeled "±0.04 m/s² (95% CI, partial derivative propagation)" — the same measurement expressed as a distribution with explicit confidence bounds and method noted. A caption beneath both panels reads: "One of these is an observation. The other is an engineering claim." Black-on-white illustration, textbook diagram style with clean typography.>

**Part I of Coleman & Steele** (Chapters 1–4) is the section most relevant to E1. Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual framework: the three-way distinction between **elemental error sources** (individual contributors to uncertainty), **random errors** (scatter that reduces with repeated measurements), and **systematic errors** (bias that does not reduce with repetition). This structure and its vocabulary are exactly what Week 2's lectures develop. If a concept from lecture feels imprecise, Chapter 1 is the first place to check — it provides the careful definitions and worked examples that lecture time does not always allow.

Chapter 3 is where the **partial derivative propagation method** is fully derived. If you have seen the formula for propagating uncertainty through a derived quantity — summing the squared products of partial derivatives and input uncertainties in a root-sum-of-squares combination — that formula is the central result of this chapter. Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you will encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations — P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R — are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory.

**Chapter 4** introduces Monte Carlo simulation as a complement to the partial derivative method. Where partial derivatives give you a closed-form estimate of output uncertainty given input distributions, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from those input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of partial derivative propagation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1 — Coleman & Steele Chapter 4 is the theoretical grounding.

<image description: A side-by-side diagram comparing two uncertainty propagation methods. Left side, labeled "Partial Derivative Method": a mathematical expression showing the RSS combination of (∂f/∂x₁·u₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂·u₂)² with an arrow to a single bell curve labeled "Predicted output distribution (analytical)." Right side, labeled "Monte Carlo Simulation": an arrow from "Input distributions (sampled N=10,000 times)" flowing into a function block labeled "f(x₁, x₂, ...)" and out to a histogram of simulated outputs labeled "Empirical output distribution." A bracket below both sides reads "Both methods estimate the same quantity — check them against each other." Clean technical diagram, MATLAB-inspired plot style, CU Boulder gold accent on axis labels.>

**Part II** of Coleman & Steele addresses validation — comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but it is worth noting now: the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you will use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline.

The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate: the book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards — which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication.

---

**The Takeaway:** Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course — keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

readings.body_markdown:
# Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition
## A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously

The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution — not a number — your entire relationship with experimental data changes.

---

This reading introduces a reference text you will use repeatedly throughout this course: *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers* by Hugh W. Coleman and W. Glenn Steele, Fourth Edition. It is not a cover-to-cover assignment. It is a field guide — different chapters address different problems you will encounter, and this reading orients you to the book's structure so you can find what you need when you need it.

Coleman & Steele belongs to a tradition of engineering texts that treat measurement uncertainty not as an afterthought — the error bar you add before submitting a figure — but as the primary product of an experiment. The core thesis of the book matches the core thesis of this course: the goal of an experiment is not to produce a number, it is to produce a defensible claim. A defensible claim requires not just a result but a quantified bound on how uncertain you are about that result, derived by methods that a critical reader can evaluate and reproduce.

<image description: A two-panel illustration. Left panel shows a single point on a number line labeled "reported value: 9.81 m/s²" — clean, falsely precise, with no additional information. Right panel shows a bell curve centered on 9.81 m/s² with shaded tails labeled "±0.04 m/s² (95% CI, partial derivative propagation)" — the same measurement expressed as a distribution with explicit confidence bounds and method noted. A caption beneath both panels reads: "One of these is an observation. The other is an engineering claim." Black-on-white illustration, textbook diagram style with clean typography.>

**Part I of Coleman & Steele** (Chapters 1–4) is the section most relevant to E1. Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual framework: the three-way distinction between **elemental error sources** (individual contributors to uncertainty), **random errors** (scatter that reduces with repeated measurements), and **systematic errors** (bias that does not reduce with repetition). This structure and its vocabulary are exactly what Week 2's lectures develop. If a concept from lecture feels imprecise, Chapter 1 is the first place to check — it provides the careful definitions and worked examples that lecture time does not always allow.

Chapter 3 is where the **partial derivative propagation method** is fully derived. If you have seen the formula for propagating uncertainty through a derived quantity — summing the squared products of partial derivatives and input uncertainties in a root-sum-of-squares combination — that formula is the central result of this chapter. Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you will encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations — P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R — are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory.

**Chapter 4** introduces Monte Carlo simulation as a complement to the partial derivative method. Where partial derivatives give you a closed-form estimate of output uncertainty given input distributions, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from those input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of partial derivative propagation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1 — Coleman & Steele Chapter 4 is the theoretical grounding.

<image description: A side-by-side diagram comparing two uncertainty propagation methods. Left side, labeled "Partial Derivative Method": a mathematical expression showing the RSS combination of (∂f/∂x₁·u₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂·u₂)² with an arrow to a single bell curve labeled "Predicted output distribution (analytical)." Right side, labeled "Monte Carlo Simulation": an arrow from "Input distributions (sampled N=10,000 times)" flowing into a function block labeled "f(x₁, x₂, ...)" and out to a histogram of simulated outputs labeled "Empirical output distribution." A bracket below both sides reads "Both methods estimate the same quantity — check them against each other." Clean technical diagram, MATLAB-inspired plot style, CU Boulder gold accent on axis labels.>

**Part II** of Coleman & Steele addresses validation — comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but it is worth noting now: the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you will use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline.

The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate: the book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards — which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication.

---

**The Takeaway:** Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course — keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

variants.text:
# Technical Writing in Engineering
## Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say

The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping — not hedging, not over-explaining, not padding — and letting the evidence speak for itself.

---

You have been writing for years. You know how to tell a story, argue a point, describe something you observed. Technical writing uses none of those skills in quite the same way. It is a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason: engineering writing is not read for enjoyment. It is read by someone who needs to make a decision, verify a result, reproduce a measurement, or check your work. Every sentence exists to support that purpose, or it should not exist at all.

This reading gives you the practical framework for engineering writing that you will apply throughout this course — starting with the Lab 1 brief report this week.

---

## The Reader You Are Writing For

Before writing a single sentence, ask yourself: *who reads this, and what do they need from it?*

In professional engineering, your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent — they understand the physics, the methods, the vocabulary. They are not, however, clairvoyant. They cannot infer what you measured from how you describe your setup. They cannot reconstruct your uncertainty calculation from a result with no error bars. They cannot evaluate your conclusion without knowing how you got there.

**Write for the skeptical expert, not the sympathetic friend.** The sympathetic friend fills in gaps; the skeptical expert points at them. The goal is a document that survives the skeptical expert: one where every claim is supported, every result is reproducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential.

A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer. Several assignments in this course ask you to explain a technical result to someone without an engineering background. This is not dumbing down — it is a different technical skill. Plain-language communication requires you to identify the *essential idea* underneath the technical apparatus and express it without jargon. If you cannot do that, it is a signal that your own understanding is not yet complete.

<image description: A two-panel illustration. Left panel, labeled "The Sympathetic Friend," shows a reader leaning forward, nodding, filling in a thought bubble with a complete version of an incomplete sentence: the written text trails off mid-sentence but the reader's thought bubble finishes it generously. Right panel, labeled "The Skeptical Expert," shows a reader with a red pen, question marks in their thought bubble, circling the same incomplete sentence and writing "Insufficient detail — how was this measured?" Clean editorial illustration style, black and white with one accent color.>

---

## The Architecture of a Technical Document

Every technical document — lab report, memo, technical note — is a narrative. Not a story with plot and character, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without clear structure is a document that wastes the reader's time.

The standard structure for an engineering report maps directly onto the logical arc of a technical argument:

**Introduction** — Three things, in this order: (1) what this document is about and why it was written; (2) what new information it contains that the reader did not have before; (3) why the results matter. The introduction is not background for its own sake — it is a contract with the reader about what they are about to receive.

**Body** — The evidence: your method, your measurements, your analysis. Sufficient detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live — each one integrated into the narrative, not appended to it.

**Conclusion** — Not a summary of the introduction. A conclusion states: (1) what was found; (2) what that finding implies; (3) what remains uncertain or unresolved; (4) what comes next. Conclusions that merely restate the introduction have done nothing.

If a section of your report does not serve one of these three purposes, it probably should not be there.

---

## Figures: The Core Unit of Technical Communication

Most technical writing errors are not in the prose — they are in the figures and in the prose that surrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific claim, and every element of the figure must serve that claim.

When you produce any figure in this course, the following elements are required — not optional:

- **Labeled axes with units.** "Force (N)" not "Force." "Time (s)" not "seconds." No axis is unlabeled, no unit is omitted.
- **A caption that identifies and explains.** The caption must name every plotted element and explain what the figure shows. "Figure 2. Measured power vs. voltage for all three configurations" is a label. "Figure 2. Measured power as a function of applied voltage for the P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." — that is a caption.
- **Reference in the text.** Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." — never a figure floating in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there.
- **Conclusions drawn explicitly.** Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation.

<image description: Two versions of the same MATLAB-style scatter plot with a best-fit line. Top version, labeled "Insufficient," has unlabeled axes ("x" and "y"), no units, a title that says "Data," and no caption below. Bottom version, labeled "Sufficient," has the same data but with fully labeled axes ("Applied Voltage (V)" and "Measured Power (W)"), a legend, error bars on data points, a figure number, and a two-sentence caption that names the trend and identifies what the error bars represent. Same data, entirely different level of communication. Clean technical diagram style.>

---

## The Language of Technical Writing

Engineering writing has a distinctive voice — and it is not stuffy or passive by accident. The conventions exist because precision matters more than personality.

**No contractions.** "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not "can't." This is not pedantry — contractions are a signal of informality that undermines technical credibility in a professional document.

**Define before you use.** Every acronym is spelled out on first use: "National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)." Every non-obvious symbol is defined. Assume the reader can look up a word; do not assume they will know what your variable names mean.

**Be specific.** Vague claims invite skepticism. "The measurement was close to the predicted value" tells the reader nothing useful. "The measured power (4.32 W) fell within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim. Every quantitative result should be stated with its uncertainty in engineering form: *estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level).*

**Active voice, lean sentences.** "The apparatus was connected, and three sets of data were collected" is cleaner than "I plugged in the apparatus and successfully collected three sets of data." The passive voice is appropriate when the actor is irrelevant or unknown — not as a default. The goal is directness, not impersonality.

**Remove what does not earn its place.** Technical writing is edited ruthlessly. Words like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length without adding meaning. The revision process is not about polishing prose — it is about cutting everything that does not serve the reader's need to understand your result.

---

## Numbers, Equations, and Units

Numbers and equations follow conventions that are enforced uniformly in professional engineering writing — and will be enforced in this course.

- **Space between number and unit.** Write `9.81 m/s²` not `9.81m/s²`. Write `2.4 mm` not `2.4mm`. No exceptions.
- **Consistent units.** Do not switch between metric and imperial mid-document. If your data is in N, your results are in N.
- **Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not.** The variable *P* represents power; the value 4.32 W is a measurement. Only variables are italicized.
- **Equations are part of the text.** Introduce every equation in a sentence: "Power dissipated in the resistor is given by..." Equations are not standalone objects — they are statements that the surrounding prose must set up and interpret.
- **Every result carries uncertainty.** A number without uncertainty bounds is not an engineering result — it is a reading. State method, confidence level, and bounds every time.

---

## The Revision Mindset

Good technical writing is not written — it is revised. The document you produce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional communicator — engineers included — treats revision as the actual work.

A practical three-pass approach: **First pass**, check structure — does the document have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion that form a complete logical argument? **Second pass**, check claims — is every quantitative statement supported? Is every figure correctly referenced and captioned? Is every conclusion drawn explicitly from evidence? **Third pass**, check language — are there contractions? Vague terms? Redundant words? Sentences that could be cut in half without losing meaning?

This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework — it intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may have perfect grammar and confident tone while being subtly wrong about the physics, imprecise about the uncertainty, or logically disconnected in its conclusions. **Your job is not to produce the draft — it is to know what right looks like so you can recognize when the draft is wrong.** The revision mindset is the professional skill this course is building.

<image description: A horizontal flow diagram with three labeled stages. Stage 1, "Draft," shows a rough document icon with a thought bubble that says "organized my ideas." Stage 2, "Revision," shows a document with red ink marks, strikethroughs, and margin notes — labels point to specific types of edits: "structural," "claims," "language." Stage 3, "Final," shows a clean document icon with a checkmark. An arrow beneath all three stages reads: "The revision IS the work." Flat illustration style, clean typography, CU Boulder gold accent color on the arrow.>

---

## A Checklist for Every Deliverable

Before submitting any written work in this course, confirm:

- [ ] Every figure has labeled axes with units, a complete caption, and an explicit reference in the text
- [ ] Every quantitative result is stated in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level)
- [ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance
- [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications, uncertainties, and next steps — not a restatement of the introduction
- [ ] No contractions appear anywhere in the document
- [ ] Every acronym is defined on first use; every symbol is defined before use
- [ ] There is a space between every number and its unit
- [ ] Units are consistent throughout
- [ ] Every claim that needs evidence has it
- [ ] The document has been read aloud at least once — sentences that are hard to say aloud are hard to read

---

**The Takeaway:** Technical writing is not a soft skill bolted onto engineering — it is the mechanism by which your results become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a result that does not yet exist as engineering knowledge.

---

*CLO Alignment: CLO 5 — Communicate experimental results effectively using standard technical writing conventions*
*Assigned: E1, Week 2 | Companion deliverable: Lab 1 Brief Report*
*Sources: Write More Good Club Technical Writing Checklist (UTEP/Rumpf Lab); Technical Writer's Checklist, TSC 2007; ASEN 2003 Lab Report Guide (Scheeres/Park, CU Boulder)*
