variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The five elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists.

================================================================================

slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters
(Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?")
Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong.

================================================================================

slide 3: The Five Elements of a Defensible Prediction
(Image: A vertical numbered list with five labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Below all five: "All five are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.")
Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all five of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. And the named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. Five elements. All five are required.

================================================================================

slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features
(Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.")
Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable."

================================================================================

slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs
(Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.")
Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. This is the predict-from-specs workflow you practiced at the end of the Week 4 lab: given a spec sheet, apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation, and report the predicted output uncertainty. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now you have not run the experiment yourself — you are working from the spec sheet included in the experiment packet. The calculation is the same: take the sensor accuracies and resolutions from the spec, propagate them through the governing equation, rank the contributions, and report the dominant source. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs.

================================================================================

slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch
(Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 60. Y-axis labeled "Temperature (°C)" from 20 to 100. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.")
Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first.

================================================================================

slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion
(Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.")
Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful.

================================================================================

slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower
(Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Scores lower than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.")
Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing and the grading reflects that. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins.

================================================================================

slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment
(Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.)
Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction.

================================================================================

slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session
(Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.")
Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all five elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, and write the acceptance criterion. Then write the risk assessment section: identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate model-form failure in this experiment. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The five elements of a defensible prediction are the five components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now.

================================================================================

lectures.body_markdown:
E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The five elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists.

================================================================================

slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters
(Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?")
Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong.

================================================================================

slide 3: The Five Elements of a Defensible Prediction
(Image: A vertical numbered list with five labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Below all five: "All five are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.")
Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all five of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. And the named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. Five elements. All five are required.

================================================================================

slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features
(Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.")
Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable."

================================================================================

slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs
(Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.")
Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. This is the predict-from-specs workflow you practiced at the end of the Week 4 lab: given a spec sheet, apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation, and report the predicted output uncertainty. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now you have not run the experiment yourself — you are working from the spec sheet included in the experiment packet. The calculation is the same: take the sensor accuracies and resolutions from the spec, propagate them through the governing equation, rank the contributions, and report the dominant source. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs.

================================================================================

slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch
(Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 60. Y-axis labeled "Temperature (°C)" from 20 to 100. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.")
Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first.

================================================================================

slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion
(Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.")
Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful.

================================================================================

slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower
(Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Scores lower than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.")
Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing and the grading reflects that. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins.

================================================================================

slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment
(Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.)
Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction.

================================================================================

slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session
(Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.")
Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all five elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, and write the acceptance criterion. Then write the risk assessment section: identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate model-form failure in this experiment. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The five elements of a defensible prediction are the five components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now.

================================================================================

variants.text:
E1_W5_L2 — The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 2
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough
Talking Point: This lecture is not a slide lecture in the usual sense. What I am going to do is walk through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I am going to do it transparently, in real time, narrating every decision I make — including the ones I am uncertain about. I will not polish this demonstration. I will show you the thinking, not just the result, because the thinking is what you are learning to do. When I reveal the data and we compare it to the prediction, I will tell you exactly what the discrepancy means and which category of disagreement it falls into. By the end of this lecture, you will have seen the complete Phase 2 workflow executed once — and E1 will be closed.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Experiment — A Torsional Pendulum
(Image: A schematic diagram of a torsional pendulum. A horizontal disk is suspended by a thin metal rod or wire attached at its center, which is fixed at the top to a rigid support. The disk is shown mid-oscillation with angular displacement θ indicated by an arrow. Labels: "Disk: moment of inertia I (kg·m²)," "Suspension wire: torsional stiffness κ (N·m/rad)," "Angular position sensor: encoder at top of wire." A second label identifies the equipment as an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that no student in the room ran as Phase 1. A text note: "No one in this room ran this experiment. I am working from the theory primer and the sensor spec sheet — exactly what you have for your Phase 2 packet.")
Talking Point: The experiment is a torsional pendulum. A disk is suspended by a thin rod with known torsional stiffness. When the disk is displaced angularly and released, it oscillates — and the oscillation frequency depends on the torsional stiffness of the rod and the moment of inertia of the disk. This is an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that none of you ran as your Phase 1. I have the same materials you have for Phase 2: a theory primer that describes the experiment and gives the governing equation, and a sensor spec sheet for the angular encoder used to measure the oscillation. I have not seen the data. What I am about to do is walk through the five elements of a prediction using those materials alone — exactly the workflow from this morning's lecture.

================================================================================

slide 3: Step 1 — Extract the Governing Equation and System Response Features
(Image: A derivation slide. The governing equation: "I·θ̈ + κ·θ = 0," leading to the natural frequency expression: "ωₙ = √(κ/I)" and the period "T = 2π√(I/κ)." Below: a table showing the values from the theory primer: "I = 0.0248 kg·m² (disk geometry, measured with calipers and a scale)," "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad (from wire calibration data in the packet)." Calculation: "T = 2π√(0.0248/0.0315) = 2π × 0.887 = 5.57 s." A gold box: "System Response Feature: The torsional pendulum period is predicted to be 5.57 s. The oscillation amplitude should decay due to air damping — but the model assumes undamped oscillation. Flag this assumption explicitly.")
Talking Point: Step one: read the theory primer and extract the governing equation. For a torsional pendulum, the equation of motion is I times theta double-dot plus kappa times theta equals zero, giving a natural frequency of the square root of kappa over I. With the values from the primer — moment of inertia 0.0248 kilogram-meter-squared, torsional stiffness 0.0315 Newton-meters per radian — the predicted period is 5.57 seconds. That is the system response feature I am committing to. I also note immediately that the model assumes undamped oscillation — there is no damping term. The actual disk oscillates in air, which has some drag. This means I should expect the measured period to be slightly longer than the undamped prediction, and the amplitude to decay. That is my first model-form assumption flagged before I see the data.

================================================================================

slide 4: Step 2 — Propagate Uncertainty from the Spec Sheet
(Image: A partial derivative propagation table. Column headers: "Parameter," "Value," "Partial Derivative ∂T/∂(param)," "Uncertainty δ(param)," "Contribution |∂T/∂(param) × δ(param)|." Rows: "I = 0.0248 kg·m²" — partial derivative = π/√(κI) — δI = ±5% from geometry measurement — contribution = ±0.14 s. "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad" — partial derivative = −π√(I)/κ^(3/2) — δκ = ±8% from wire calibration uncertainty — contribution = ±0.22 s. "Combined uncertainty (quadrature sum): δT = √(0.14² + 0.22²) = ±0.26 s." A gold box: "Predicted period: 5.57 ± 0.26 s (95% CI, partial derivative method). Dominant source: torsional stiffness κ, contributing 72% of total output variance.")
Talking Point: Step two: propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet values. I take the partial derivative of the period with respect to I and with respect to kappa. The stiffness kappa has larger uncertainty than the moment of inertia — the wire calibration in the packet carries 8% uncertainty, while the geometry measurement for I carries about 5%. Evaluating the propagation, the kappa term dominates: it contributes 72% of the total output variance. The combined uncertainty in the predicted period is plus or minus 0.26 seconds at 95% confidence. So my prediction is: the period is 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. That is a specific, defensible claim derived from the spec sheet — without having seen the angular encoder measure a single oscillation.

================================================================================

slide 5: Step 3 — The Committed Sketch
(Image: A hand-drawn-style time series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 30. Y-axis labeled "Angular Displacement θ (rad)" from −0.8 to 0.8. The expected oscillation is shown as a sinusoidal wave with a period of approximately 5.57 seconds. An uncertainty band shades the region within ±0.26 s of the expected period — shown as a slight smearing of the zero crossings. A separate pair of dashed lines shows gradual amplitude decay due to air damping: "Expected amplitude envelope — decays slowly due to air drag (not modeled, but physically expected)." Axes are labeled with units. A gold annotation at the first period: "T = 5.57 ± 0.26 s — committed before data release." The plot is clearly labeled as a pre-data sketch.)
Talking Point: Step three: draw the committed sketch. Here is the expected oscillation — sinusoidal, period 5.57 seconds, amplitude starting near the initial displacement and decaying slowly. I have drawn the amplitude envelope with a slight decay because I know air drag will be present even though the model ignores it. The shaded region at the zero crossings represents the uncertainty in the period — if the measured zero crossings are within the shaded band, the period is consistent with the prediction. If they are systematically outside, I have a discrepancy. Notice that I have committed to the shape, the period, the approximate initial amplitude, and the expected slow decay — all before seeing a single data point. This is the sketch that will go into my Phase 2 memo, and it is the first thing I will overlay the data on when it comes in.

================================================================================

slide 6: Step 4 — Acceptance Criterion and Risk Assessment
(Image: Two clearly separated text boxes. Box 1 — "Acceptance Criterion": "The prediction is successful if the measured oscillation period is within 5.57 ± 0.26 s (the propagated uncertainty interval at 95% CI). A measured period outside this interval requires a physical explanation — either an unaccounted-for uncertainty source or a model-form limitation." Box 2 — "Risk Assessment — Two Anomalous Signatures to Watch": "(1) If the period decreases significantly with amplitude (nonlinearity), the small-angle approximation in the model has failed. The linear model is invalid at large angular displacements. (2) If the period is significantly shorter than predicted — less than 5.2 s — the torsional stiffness in the spec may be miscalibrated, or the wire has hardened since the calibration was performed." Both boxes use consistent formatting. Caption: "Commit to what wrong looks like before you see the data.")
Talking Point: Step four: state the acceptance criterion and the risk assessment. The acceptance criterion is numerical and pre-committed — if the measured period is within 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds, I will classify the comparison as supporting agreement. If it falls outside, I need a physical explanation. The risk assessment names two specific anomalous patterns I should watch for. First: if the measured period decreases with amplitude — if early oscillations are shorter than later ones as the amplitude decays — the small-angle approximation in the linearized model has failed. The linear model is only valid for small angular displacements, and this failure is predictable in advance. Second: if the measured period is significantly shorter than predicted — say, below 5.2 seconds — the torsional stiffness in the packet may be wrong, or the wire properties have changed since calibration. Both anomalous signatures are physically motivated, and both are commitable before the data is released.

================================================================================

slide 7: Data Release — What Did We Measure?
(Image: A plot of actual measured angular displacement vs. time for the torsional pendulum experiment. The time axis runs from 0 to 30 seconds. The oscillation is clearly sinusoidal, and the amplitude decays slowly as expected. A highlighted annotation marks the measured period at several zero crossings — averaging to approximately 5.83 seconds. The committed prediction range (5.31 to 5.83 s) is shown as a vertical shaded band at the boundary — 5.83 is at the outer edge of the prediction interval. The amplitude decay matches the sketched envelope approximately. A gold annotation at the measured period: "Measured: T = 5.83 ± 0.05 s (from zero-crossing analysis, 8 oscillations)." The figure is clearly labeled as the actual data, in contrast to the sketch from slide 5.)
Talking Point: Here is the data. The measured period, averaged over eight complete oscillations using zero-crossing analysis, is 5.83 seconds. My prediction was 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. The measured value is at the very edge of my prediction interval — 5.83 is the upper bound of 5.57 plus 0.26. So: does this constitute agreement? Using my pre-committed criterion — agreement if the measured period is within the uncertainty interval — the answer is marginal. 5.83 is exactly at the boundary. I need to apply the validation metric carefully. The relative difference is (5.83 minus 5.57) divided by 5.57 — about 4.7%. The combined uncertainty is 0.26 s, which is 4.7% of the model prediction. So the difference is just barely within the combined uncertainty. I will classify this as marginal agreement — but I should investigate the discrepancy rather than simply accepting agreement.

================================================================================

slide 8: What Does the Discrepancy Tell Us?
(Image: A residual analysis plot. X-axis: "Oscillation Number" from 1 to 8. Y-axis: "Period (s) for each oscillation." The individual measured periods are plotted as dots. A horizontal dashed line marks the predicted value of 5.57 s. All measured periods are above the prediction line, and there is a slight trend: earlier oscillations (when amplitude is large) have slightly longer periods — around 5.90 s — while later oscillations (when amplitude is small) converge toward 5.65 s. A gold annotation: "Systematic trend: period decreases as amplitude decreases — consistent with nonlinear stiffness at large displacement." A second annotation: "Residuals are NOT random. The small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement — this was the first risk assessment item.")
Talking Point: Look at the residuals. The measured period is not just slightly high — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later oscillations, as amplitude decays, have shorter periods approaching the linear model prediction. That is not random scatter. That is the small-angle approximation failing at large displacement — exactly the first risk assessment item I committed to before seeing the data. The linear model predicts a constant period regardless of amplitude. The physical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for small theta. At large theta, the nonlinear sine term in the full restoring force equation matters. The anomalous signature I predicted is the one I see. This is what it looks like when a model-form failure is predicted in advance and then confirmed: you learn something specific about the physics of the system, not just that the model was approximately right.

================================================================================

slide 9: Classifying the Discrepancy
(Image: A three-category taxonomy table, referencing the taxonomy introduced in the W5 Reading "Discrepancy Is Data." Three rows: Category 1: "Within propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. No model revision required." Status for this experiment: "Not applicable — residuals show systematic trend, not random scatter." Category 2: "Larger than uncertainty band, no systematic structure — suggests underestimated uncertainty." Status for this experiment: "Partially applicable — period is at the edge of the uncertainty band." Category 3: "Systematic trend in residuals — model-form limitation. Model missing a physical effect." Status for this experiment: "PRIMARY CATEGORY — amplitude-dependent period is consistent with small-angle approximation failure." A gold box: "Classification: Category 3 — model-form limitation. The model is missing the nonlinear restoring force term active at large displacement." Caption: "Category 3 is the most informative discrepancy — it tells you what physics to add to the model.")
Talking Point: Let me classify this discrepancy using the taxonomy from the reading assigned this week. Category 1 is scatter within the uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. That does not apply here: the residuals are structured, not random. Category 2 is a gap larger than the uncertainty band with no systematic structure — suggesting underestimated uncertainty. That is partially applicable, since the measured period is at the edge of our interval. But the primary classification is Category 3: a systematic trend in the residuals that reveals a model-form limitation. The amplitude-dependent period is direct evidence that the small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement. The correct physical model would use the full nonlinear equation of motion — or at minimum, the perturbation correction for finite amplitude oscillations. Category 3 is the most informative kind of discrepancy because it tells you specifically what physics to add to the model. The experimentally informed model revision is clear: include the amplitude correction.

================================================================================

slide 10: What This Closes — And What It Opens
(Image: A split slide. Left half: "What Phase 1 built." A horizontal list: "AMVF literacy," "Measurement fundamentals," "Uncertainty propagation," "Quantitative comparison," "Full pipeline execution," "Peer critique." Label: "You can walk the physical modeling branch." Right half: "What Phase 2 revealed." A horizontal list: "Prediction from specs alone," "Pre-commitment," "Anomalous signature prediction," "Discrepancy classification." Label: "You can engage both branches." Below both halves, a bridge spanning the full width labeled "Transition to Phase II — E2." Text: "In E2, prediction before measurement is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The models are more complex. The stakes are higher. The cognitive move is the same." A closing question centered at the bottom: "What would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?")
Talking Point: Let me close E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In Phase 1, you walked the physical modeling branch — you measured, you propagated uncertainty, you compared. Phase 2 added the pre-commitment step — before seeing the data, you built a prediction from equations and specs alone. What you practiced in Phase 2 is the same skill E2 requires, applied under higher stakes. In E2, the canned experiments are more complex aerospace systems — more parameters, more coupled physical effects, more places for model assumptions to fail. The models require more careful reading. The uncertainty budgets are harder to construct from a spec sheet alone. And crucially, prediction before hardware access is not optional in E2 — it is the gate. You submit your prediction memo, and only after submission does the experiment begin. The question that opens E2 is the same question we just answered for the torsional pendulum: what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend? That is the question of a test engineer. That is the question you are now equipped to answer.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
E1 is complete. Over the last five weeks, you walked the right branch of the AMVF — from hypothesis through measurement through uncertainty propagation through quantitative comparison — and you finished by committing to a prediction before seeing data and evaluating that prediction against the outcome. The torsional pendulum example in this lecture showed you what that looks like when it is done transparently: every decision narrated, every assumption named, every discrepancy classified and physically interpreted. The discrepancy was not a failure. It was the most valuable outcome of the experiment — a specific, physically interpretable model-form limitation that tells you exactly what to improve. That is what a test engineer produces. That is what experimental methods is for. The rest of this course builds that skill into more complex systems, more demanding modeling requirements, and eventually your own experimental design from scratch. You are ready for what comes next.

================================================================================

E1_W5_L2 — The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 2
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough
Talking Point: This lecture is not a slide lecture in the usual sense. What I am going to do is walk through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I am going to do it transparently, in real time, narrating every decision I make — including the ones I am uncertain about. I will not polish this demonstration. I will show you the thinking, not just the result, because the thinking is what you are learning to do. When I reveal the data and we compare it to the prediction, I will tell you exactly what the discrepancy means and which category of disagreement it falls into. By the end of this lecture, you will have seen the complete Phase 2 workflow executed once — and E1 will be closed.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Experiment — A Torsional Pendulum
(Image: A schematic diagram of a torsional pendulum. A horizontal disk is suspended by a thin metal rod or wire attached at its center, which is fixed at the top to a rigid support. The disk is shown mid-oscillation with angular displacement θ indicated by an arrow. Labels: "Disk: moment of inertia I (kg·m²)," "Suspension wire: torsional stiffness κ (N·m/rad)," "Angular position sensor: encoder at top of wire." A second label identifies the equipment as an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that no student in the room ran as Phase 1. A text note: "No one in this room ran this experiment. I am working from the theory primer and the sensor spec sheet — exactly what you have for your Phase 2 packet.")
Talking Point: The experiment is a torsional pendulum. A disk is suspended by a thin rod with known torsional stiffness. When the disk is displaced angularly and released, it oscillates — and the oscillation frequency depends on the torsional stiffness of the rod and the moment of inertia of the disk. This is an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that none of you ran as your Phase 1. I have the same materials you have for Phase 2: a theory primer that describes the experiment and gives the governing equation, and a sensor spec sheet for the angular encoder used to measure the oscillation. I have not seen the data. What I am about to do is walk through the five elements of a prediction using those materials alone — exactly the workflow from this morning's lecture.

================================================================================

slide 3: Step 1 — Extract the Governing Equation and System Response Features
(Image: A derivation slide. The governing equation: "I·θ̈ + κ·θ = 0," leading to the natural frequency expression: "ωₙ = √(κ/I)" and the period "T = 2π√(I/κ)." Below: a table showing the values from the theory primer: "I = 0.0248 kg·m² (disk geometry, measured with calipers and a scale)," "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad (from wire calibration data in the packet)." Calculation: "T = 2π√(0.0248/0.0315) = 2π × 0.887 = 5.57 s." A gold box: "System Response Feature: The torsional pendulum period is predicted to be 5.57 s. The oscillation amplitude should decay due to air damping — but the model assumes undamped oscillation. Flag this assumption explicitly.")
Talking Point: Step one: read the theory primer and extract the governing equation. For a torsional pendulum, the equation of motion is I times theta double-dot plus kappa times theta equals zero, giving a natural frequency of the square root of kappa over I. With the values from the primer — moment of inertia 0.0248 kilogram-meter-squared, torsional stiffness 0.0315 Newton-meters per radian — the predicted period is 5.57 seconds. That is the system response feature I am committing to. I also note immediately that the model assumes undamped oscillation — there is no damping term. The actual disk oscillates in air, which has some drag. This means I should expect the measured period to be slightly longer than the undamped prediction, and the amplitude to decay. That is my first model-form assumption flagged before I see the data.

================================================================================

slide 4: Step 2 — Propagate Uncertainty from the Spec Sheet
(Image: A partial derivative propagation table. Column headers: "Parameter," "Value," "Partial Derivative ∂T/∂(param)," "Uncertainty δ(param)," "Contribution |∂T/∂(param) × δ(param)|." Rows: "I = 0.0248 kg·m²" — partial derivative = π/√(κI) — δI = ±5% from geometry measurement — contribution = ±0.14 s. "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad" — partial derivative = −π√(I)/κ^(3/2) — δκ = ±8% from wire calibration uncertainty — contribution = ±0.22 s. "Combined uncertainty (quadrature sum): δT = √(0.14² + 0.22²) = ±0.26 s." A gold box: "Predicted period: 5.57 ± 0.26 s (95% CI, partial derivative method). Dominant source: torsional stiffness κ, contributing 72% of total output variance.")
Talking Point: Step two: propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet values. I take the partial derivative of the period with respect to I and with respect to kappa. The stiffness kappa has larger uncertainty than the moment of inertia — the wire calibration in the packet carries 8% uncertainty, while the geometry measurement for I carries about 5%. Evaluating the propagation, the kappa term dominates: it contributes 72% of the total output variance. The combined uncertainty in the predicted period is plus or minus 0.26 seconds at 95% confidence. So my prediction is: the period is 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. That is a specific, defensible claim derived from the spec sheet — without having seen the angular encoder measure a single oscillation.

================================================================================

slide 5: Step 3 — The Committed Sketch
(Image: A hand-drawn-style time series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 30. Y-axis labeled "Angular Displacement θ (rad)" from −0.8 to 0.8. The expected oscillation is shown as a sinusoidal wave with a period of approximately 5.57 seconds. An uncertainty band shades the region within ±0.26 s of the expected period — shown as a slight smearing of the zero crossings. A separate pair of dashed lines shows gradual amplitude decay due to air damping: "Expected amplitude envelope — decays slowly due to air drag (not modeled, but physically expected)." Axes are labeled with units. A gold annotation at the first period: "T = 5.57 ± 0.26 s — committed before data release." The plot is clearly labeled as a pre-data sketch.)
Talking Point: Step three: draw the committed sketch. Here is the expected oscillation — sinusoidal, period 5.57 seconds, amplitude starting near the initial displacement and decaying slowly. I have drawn the amplitude envelope with a slight decay because I know air drag will be present even though the model ignores it. The shaded region at the zero crossings represents the uncertainty in the period — if the measured zero crossings are within the shaded band, the period is consistent with the prediction. If they are systematically outside, I have a discrepancy. Notice that I have committed to the shape, the period, the approximate initial amplitude, and the expected slow decay — all before seeing a single data point. This is the sketch that will go into my Phase 2 memo, and it is the first thing I will overlay the data on when it comes in.

================================================================================

slide 6: Step 4 — Acceptance Criterion and Risk Assessment
(Image: Two clearly separated text boxes. Box 1 — "Acceptance Criterion": "The prediction is successful if the measured oscillation period is within 5.57 ± 0.26 s (the propagated uncertainty interval at 95% CI). A measured period outside this interval requires a physical explanation — either an unaccounted-for uncertainty source or a model-form limitation." Box 2 — "Risk Assessment — Two Anomalous Signatures to Watch": "(1) If the period decreases significantly with amplitude (nonlinearity), the small-angle approximation in the model has failed. The linear model is invalid at large angular displacements. (2) If the period is significantly shorter than predicted — less than 5.2 s — the torsional stiffness in the spec may be miscalibrated, or the wire has hardened since the calibration was performed." Both boxes use consistent formatting. Caption: "Commit to what wrong looks like before you see the data.")
Talking Point: Step four: state the acceptance criterion and the risk assessment. The acceptance criterion is numerical and pre-committed — if the measured period is within 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds, I will classify the comparison as supporting agreement. If it falls outside, I need a physical explanation. The risk assessment names two specific anomalous patterns I should watch for. First: if the measured period decreases with amplitude — if early oscillations are shorter than later ones as the amplitude decays — the small-angle approximation in the linearized model has failed. The linear model is only valid for small angular displacements, and this failure is predictable in advance. Second: if the measured period is significantly shorter than predicted — say, below 5.2 seconds — the torsional stiffness in the packet may be wrong, or the wire properties have changed since calibration. Both anomalous signatures are physically motivated, and both are commitable before the data is released.

================================================================================

slide 7: Data Release — What Did We Measure?
(Image: A plot of actual measured angular displacement vs. time for the torsional pendulum experiment. The time axis runs from 0 to 30 seconds. The oscillation is clearly sinusoidal, and the amplitude decays slowly as expected. A highlighted annotation marks the measured period at several zero crossings — averaging to approximately 5.83 seconds. The committed prediction range (5.31 to 5.83 s) is shown as a vertical shaded band at the boundary — 5.83 is at the outer edge of the prediction interval. The amplitude decay matches the sketched envelope approximately. A gold annotation at the measured period: "Measured: T = 5.83 ± 0.05 s (from zero-crossing analysis, 8 oscillations)." The figure is clearly labeled as the actual data, in contrast to the sketch from slide 5.)
Talking Point: Here is the data. The measured period, averaged over eight complete oscillations using zero-crossing analysis, is 5.83 seconds. My prediction was 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. The measured value is at the very edge of my prediction interval — 5.83 is the upper bound of 5.57 plus 0.26. So: does this constitute agreement? Using my pre-committed criterion — agreement if the measured period is within the uncertainty interval — the answer is marginal. 5.83 is exactly at the boundary. I need to apply the validation metric carefully. The relative difference is (5.83 minus 5.57) divided by 5.57 — about 4.7%. The combined uncertainty is 0.26 s, which is 4.7% of the model prediction. So the difference is just barely within the combined uncertainty. I will classify this as marginal agreement — but I should investigate the discrepancy rather than simply accepting agreement.

================================================================================

slide 8: What Does the Discrepancy Tell Us?
(Image: A residual analysis plot. X-axis: "Oscillation Number" from 1 to 8. Y-axis: "Period (s) for each oscillation." The individual measured periods are plotted as dots. A horizontal dashed line marks the predicted value of 5.57 s. All measured periods are above the prediction line, and there is a slight trend: earlier oscillations (when amplitude is large) have slightly longer periods — around 5.90 s — while later oscillations (when amplitude is small) converge toward 5.65 s. A gold annotation: "Systematic trend: period decreases as amplitude decreases — consistent with nonlinear stiffness at large displacement." A second annotation: "Residuals are NOT random. The small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement — this was the first risk assessment item.")
Talking Point: Look at the residuals. The measured period is not just slightly high — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later oscillations, as amplitude decays, have shorter periods approaching the linear model prediction. That is not random scatter. That is the small-angle approximation failing at large displacement — exactly the first risk assessment item I committed to before seeing the data. The linear model predicts a constant period regardless of amplitude. The physical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for small theta. At large theta, the nonlinear sine term in the full restoring force equation matters. The anomalous signature I predicted is the one I see. This is what it looks like when a model-form failure is predicted in advance and then confirmed: you learn something specific about the physics of the system, not just that the model was approximately right.

================================================================================

slide 9: Classifying the Discrepancy
(Image: A three-category taxonomy table, referencing the taxonomy introduced in the W5 Reading "Discrepancy Is Data." Three rows: Category 1: "Within propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. No model revision required." Status for this experiment: "Not applicable — residuals show systematic trend, not random scatter." Category 2: "Larger than uncertainty band, no systematic structure — suggests underestimated uncertainty." Status for this experiment: "Partially applicable — period is at the edge of the uncertainty band." Category 3: "Systematic trend in residuals — model-form limitation. Model missing a physical effect." Status for this experiment: "PRIMARY CATEGORY — amplitude-dependent period is consistent with small-angle approximation failure." A gold box: "Classification: Category 3 — model-form limitation. The model is missing the nonlinear restoring force term active at large displacement." Caption: "Category 3 is the most informative discrepancy — it tells you what physics to add to the model.")
Talking Point: Let me classify this discrepancy using the taxonomy from the reading assigned this week. Category 1 is scatter within the uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. That does not apply here: the residuals are structured, not random. Category 2 is a gap larger than the uncertainty band with no systematic structure — suggesting underestimated uncertainty. That is partially applicable, since the measured period is at the edge of our interval. But the primary classification is Category 3: a systematic trend in the residuals that reveals a model-form limitation. The amplitude-dependent period is direct evidence that the small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement. The correct physical model would use the full nonlinear equation of motion — or at minimum, the perturbation correction for finite amplitude oscillations. Category 3 is the most informative kind of discrepancy because it tells you specifically what physics to add to the model. The experimentally informed model revision is clear: include the amplitude correction.

================================================================================

slide 10: What This Closes — And What It Opens
(Image: A split slide. Left half: "What Phase 1 built." A horizontal list: "AMVF literacy," "Measurement fundamentals," "Uncertainty propagation," "Quantitative comparison," "Full pipeline execution," "Peer critique." Label: "You can walk the physical modeling branch." Right half: "What Phase 2 revealed." A horizontal list: "Prediction from specs alone," "Pre-commitment," "Anomalous signature prediction," "Discrepancy classification." Label: "You can engage both branches." Below both halves, a bridge spanning the full width labeled "Transition to Phase II — E2." Text: "In E2, prediction before measurement is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The models are more complex. The stakes are higher. The cognitive move is the same." A closing question centered at the bottom: "What would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?")
Talking Point: Let me close E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In Phase 1, you walked the physical modeling branch — you measured, you propagated uncertainty, you compared. Phase 2 added the pre-commitment step — before seeing the data, you built a prediction from equations and specs alone. What you practiced in Phase 2 is the same skill E2 requires, applied under higher stakes. In E2, the canned experiments are more complex aerospace systems — more parameters, more coupled physical effects, more places for model assumptions to fail. The models require more careful reading. The uncertainty budgets are harder to construct from a spec sheet alone. And crucially, prediction before hardware access is not optional in E2 — it is the gate. You submit your prediction memo, and only after submission does the experiment begin. The question that opens E2 is the same question we just answered for the torsional pendulum: what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend? That is the question of a test engineer. That is the question you are now equipped to answer.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
E1 is complete. Over the last five weeks, you walked the right branch of the AMVF — from hypothesis through measurement through uncertainty propagation through quantitative comparison — and you finished by committing to a prediction before seeing data and evaluating that prediction against the outcome. The torsional pendulum example in this lecture showed you what that looks like when it is done transparently: every decision narrated, every assumption named, every discrepancy classified and physically interpreted. The discrepancy was not a failure. It was the most valuable outcome of the experiment — a specific, physically interpretable model-form limitation that tells you exactly what to improve. That is what a test engineer produces. That is what experimental methods is for. The rest of this course builds that skill into more complex systems, more demanding modeling requirements, and eventually your own experimental design from scratch.
